title: Why Scoot Henderson Can Still Break Out Despite Portland’s Crowded Backcourt
description: Ja Morant and Damian Lillard may complicate the Trail Blazers’ rotation, but they could also create the ideal environment for Scoot Henderson to take the next step.
fullText:
The Portland Trail Blazers will have a chance to once again be a playoff team next season, especially after adding Ja Morant to the mix. But that acquisition also creates a natural question.

What does it mean for Scoot Henderson?

That question becomes even more complicated with Damian Lillard expected to return from injury. Between Morant, Lillard, Henderson, Shaedon Sharpe and Jrue Holiday, Portland suddenly has one of the deepest and most crowded guard rotations in the NBA.

Even so, there is still a clear pathway for Henderson to emerge as a breakout player.

At the end of the day, cream rises. The best talent in the NBA generally finds a way to emerge. Opportunity still has to be present for that to happen, but even with all the guards on Portland’s roster, Henderson should have plenty of chances to establish himself.

In fact, the talent surrounding him could ultimately work in his favor.

Henderson will no longer be under the same pressure to serve as the primary offensive creator every time he steps onto the floor. With the amount of playmaking in Portland’s backcourt, he should have more opportunities to attack defenses that have already been forced into rotation, get downhill with space and reach the rim, where his athleticism is at its best.

It’s easy to look at the potential lack of minutes and view it as a factor working against Henderson’s breakout. But playing slightly fewer minutes in lineups that better complement his skill set could be more beneficial than carrying a larger role in an offense that asks him to manufacture everything himself.

Henderson will still have the ball in his hands. He will still have opportunities to initiate the offense and create for others. The difference is that he should not have to shoulder that responsibility on every possession.

It remains to be seen exactly what Henderson will look like within new coach Micah Nori’s system, especially with so many new pieces entering the equation. But viewing Lillard’s return and the Morant acquisition as obstacles standing in the way of Henderson’s development is not necessarily the right way to look at Portland’s situation.

Those players could make the rotation more crowded, but they should also make the game easier for Henderson. With less pressure, more playmaking around him and better opportunities to attack favorable matchups, the former No. 3 overall pick could finally be positioned to blossom into the player Portland believed he could become.

title: Sugano Returns to Pitch Rockies Past Reds
description: Tomoyuki Sugano is back from the IL and threw six innings to lead the Rockies to a 10-3 win over the Reds in Denver.
fullText:
Tomoyuki Sugano has officially resumed his role on the mound for the Colorado Rockies. The veteran right-hander made his return from the injured list to start against the Cincinnati Reds. It took him 77 pitches to complete his first appearance since being activated from the IL. Despite the sweltering heat on Saturday, Sugano navigated the lineup with ease. His solid outing helped secure a 10-3 victory over the Reds. This triumph is significant as the franchise desperately sought stability within its pitching rotation.

During this matchup, Sugano delivered an effective performance that extended into the seventh inning. He completed 6 1/3 innings while recording three strikeouts. Notably, he issued zero walks to opposing hitters. According to The Associated Press, he appeared at ease despite suffering from a sore back. This back problem was the reason he was placed on the 15-day IL earlier this month. Once on the field, the injury did not seem to hinder his movements. “I felt pretty good coming back,” Sugano said through a translator. Reports highlighted his excellent command of the fastball. Schaeffer told reporters, “Gave us just what we needed.” The manager added, “Typical Tomo Sugano stuff. He was great today.”

The sole Reds batter who managed to trouble Sugano was catcher Jose Trevino. Trevino launched a pair of solo home runs off the Japanese ace. This season, Sugano has surrendered 18 home runs in total, with 15 of those being solo shots. “Today, in particular, we were ahead by a lot of runs,” Sugano said. The offense provided him with a 6-0 cushion after just two innings. Sugano mentioned that his teammates assured him solo homers were acceptable. Nevertheless, he continues to strive for limiting damage. This marked his inaugural stint on the IL during his Major League career. Additionally, he managed a fingernail issue throughout his recovery period. Sugano improved his record to 9-4 with a 4.76 ERA in his debut season with Colorado. He has earned a win in five of his six most recent starts. The last Rockies pitcher to achieve this feat was Austin Gomber in 2021. Sugano holds a 2-0 record against the Reds this year. He previously secured a victory on April 29 by pitching 5 1/3 scoreless innings in Cincinnati.

Sugano’s comeback provides a tremendous lift for a Rockies squad that was in desperate need of it. The team counted on him to steady a rotation plagued by excessive turnover. His track record of winning five of his last six outings demonstrates he remains an elite pitcher. The 36-year-old proved capable of handling the pressure of a scorching day in Denver. His precise fastball command was exactly what the offense required to establish a lead. The fact that he issued no walks indicates his focus is razor-sharp. Although he surrendered two solo home runs, he ensured the damage remained minimal. The team’s early advantage allowed him to pitch deep into the game without added stress. This performance dispels any lingering concerns regarding his back spasms. He has demonstrated he is healthy and prepared for the long term. Supporters will feel significantly more reassured knowing he has returned to the fold. This victory against a division rival like the Reds carries added weight. It proves the Rockies can compete effectively when their key players are active.

The Rockies must prioritize keeping Sugano healthy for the remainder of the season. He needs to sustain this winning streak to assist the team in climbing the standings. Fans should monitor his pitch count closely in future starts. The organization must manage his workload carefully to prevent another trip to the IL. If he remains healthy, the Rockies have a genuine opportunity to make an impact. His success against the Reds implies he can dominate other lineups as well. The rotation appears significantly stronger with him leading the charge. Keep an eye on his next start to see if he can maintain this momentum.

title: Prominent local sports analyst names MSU AD candidate to monitor
description: Who will be the next Michigan State athletic director? A prominent local sports analyst has provided a candidate to keep an eye on moving forward
fullText:
Who will be the next Michigan State athletic director? A prominent local sports analyst has provided a candidate to keep an eye on moving forward.

For the second time in just over a year, Michigan State is looking for an athletic director. As you most likely know by now, J Batt has left after just one year with the Spartans for the same role at Kentucky, leaving Michigan State in position to find its next athletic director.

So who is Michigan State targeting? Well, Tim Staudt of WILX in Lansing has one person in mind: Dan Bartholomae of Western Michigan. Bartholomae is the current athletic director at Western Michigan, and has been a common candidate among those in the know since Batt elected to leave the Spartans.

Staudt stated that Bartholomae is some to keep an eye on, and noted that the buyout to get him from Western Michigan shouldn't be a hurdle given what Michigan State received from Kentucky in Batt's buyout. It doesn't appear that Staudt is reporting an particular intel, but given Staudt's lengthy career in the area, this kind of endorsement should be taken with serious consideration from the fan base.

Click on the post below to see the complete post from Staudt:

I’m keeping my eye on Western Michigans Dan Bartholimae as Michigan State’s next athletic director. His $5 million buyout would be mostly offset by the near $4 million MSU is getting from Kentucky for J Batt.

— Tim Staudt (@timstaudt10) July 18, 2026

title: Boston Red Sox Late Comeback Propels Winning Streak to Historic Levels
description: There is no stopping the Boston Red Sox as they stretched their winning streak to a 20-year high.
fullText:
For the first time since Opening Day in Cincinnati, the Boston Red Sox are above .500, thanks to a thrilling 7-6 win over the Tampa Bay Rays on a rainy Saturday afternoon at Fenway Park that extended their winning streak to 12.

Much like the finale in New York just six days ago, it was a sleepy start to the afternoon for the Red Sox offense, which mustered just three hits and six baserunners through the first six innings against Rays pitching.

But the tides turned in the bottom of the seventh with Tampa leading 6-3, as the Red Sox were able to capture the magic that has been with them for much of the last three weeks.

The inning began with shortstop Andruw Monasterio ripping a leadoff double off Tampa reliever Casey Legumina, chasing the right-hander from the game as Rays manager Kevin Cash turned to Cole Sulser to keep the Tampa lead at three.

Sulser began his outing against left fielder Jarren Duran, who weakly grounded a ball to first baseman Ryan Vilade; however, the first baseman was too slow getting the ball to the covering Sulser, allowing Duran to reach as Monasterio took third.

With men on the corners and still nobody out, interim manager Chad Tracy turned to Masataka Yoshida in place of catcher Carlos Narvaez, with hopes of a ball in play cutting the Tampa lead to two, 6-4. Yoshida did his job, hitting a chopper to shortstop Ben Williamson that was too slow to turn a double play on, allowing Monasterio to score and Duran to advance to second base.

With Duran on second and one out, Sulser got second baseman Anthony Seigler to strike out on four pitches, leaving it up to Ceddanne Rafaela to continue to cut into the Tampa lead. The 25-year-old All-Star did just that, launching a double off the Green Monster, scoring Duran, and cutting the lead down to just one, 6-5.

In a curious decision from Cash, the Rays skipper turned to left-hander Garrett Clevinger to face right fielder Wilyer Abreu. In 127 plate appearances, the 27-year-old Abreu has performed substantially better against left-handed pitching, hitting .345/.421/.527/.948 against southpaws in 2026.

Cash's decision proved to be exactly that as the two-time Gold Glove winner launched his fourth home run in two days, 412 feet into the bleachers, giving the Red Sox a 7-6 lead, sending the Fenway crowd into a frenzy.

With the win, the Red Sox stretch their winning streak to 12, marking the longest Red Sox winning streak since 2006. As noted by Red Sox Nation Stats on Twitter/X, they now set their sights on the franchise record, 15 straight wins, which was accomplished by the 1946 Red Sox.

They'll look to make it 13 straight on Sunday afternoon in the series finale against the Rays. Sonny Gray (11-1, 2.54 ERA) will make his first start of the unofficial second half opposite Shane McClanahan (8-5, 2.83 ERA) for Tampa Bay. First pitch from Fenway set for 1:35 p.m. ET.

Remember to join our RED SOX on ROUNDTABLE community, which is FREE! You can post your own thoughts, in text or video form, and you can engage with our Roundtable staff, as well as other Red Sox fans. If prompted to download the Roundtable APP, that's free too!

fullText:
Tommy McMillen proved he’s the real deal earlier tonight (Sat., July 18, 2026) at UFC Oklahoma LIVE on Paramount+ from inside Paycom Center in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, as the rising featherweight prospect delivered an absolute dynamite TKO (punches and knees) over hard-nosed counterpart Alberto Montes.

Outside of the main event, this fight had the most attention entering Saturday’s fight card in OKC. Both McMillen and Montes shared bad blood throughout fight week and nearly came to blows during Friday’s weigh ins. It added even more spice to an anticipated return to action for McMillen, who as most know trains alongside former UFC bantamweight champion Sean O’Malley.

Overall, this was a complete striking masterclass by McMillen. Montes had some good spots and even scored multiple knockdowns, but McMillen unloaded over 500 total strikes deep into the third round. McMillen threw a little bit of everything, including combinations from range, whipping strikes to the body, sharp elbows and knees inside, and violent upper cuts. It was a miracle that Montes remained standing, but the referee eventually saw enough and stepped in for late stoppage.

title: England's discipline 'verging on comical' - Dawson
description: Matt Dawson described England's discipline as "comical" and says they have a problem to address after receiving four yellow cards in the 31-24 win over Argentina.
fullText:
Former scrum-half Matt Dawson described England's discipline as "verging on comical" after four players were shown yellow cards in the second half of their 31-24 win over Argentina.

England were twice reduced to 13 players in Santiago del Estero, including in the final three minutes as Argentina - who themselves had three players yellow-carded - attempted to overturn a 14-point deficit to salvage a draw.

The hosts were denied a shot at levelling the match when Bautista Delguy's 83rd-minute try was controversially chalked off, after the television match official ruled Henry Slade's last-gasp tackle had forced the Argentina wing into touch.

England's gutsy victory moves them up to third in the Northern Hemisphere standings in the 2026 Nations Championship, backing up last Saturday's 73-8 win over Fiji which ended a run of five defeats.

World Cup winner Dawson defended under-fire England head coach Steve Borthwick - saying his side have shown "definite progress" this month - but believes there is a big problem with their discipline.

England have received 14 yellows and one red card in eight matches in 2026 and only once in that run (against Fiji) have they avoided having a player sin-binned.

Poor discipline was a major factor in the four-match losing run that resulted in a fifth-placed finish in the Six Nations and Borthwick's position as head coach coming under scrutiny.

"It's been a hellishly tough two weeks for England. It started with 20 minutes of hell down in South Africa, to all of a sudden finding themselves dominating Argentina in the opening 40 minutes, so there has been real progress," Dawson told BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra.

"I know the wagons are circulating around Borthwick in certain parts of the media, but I think this performance showed they are on a pathway. Whether there's enough progress to win a World Cup is another matter to be fully debated - I don't think we are there yet - but there's definite progress from the Six Nations.

"The one question we will continue to ask is what on earth are we going to do with England's discipline? It's verging on comical. It's ridiculous how many cards this team have had so far in 2026."

Borthwick hit back at questions about his team's discipline, saying: "I don't think now is the time to be digging into that.

"This team has done really, really well. It was a tough Test match, a fantastic Test match, we scored some wonderful tries and as ever you guys concentrate on the negativity.

"You're like the negativity committee so it's lovely coming and talking to you again."

England beat Argentina in thriller after late TMO call

England had four players sin-binned in 26 second-half minutes against Argentina:

Jack van Poortvliet - deliberate knock-on (51 minutes)

Alex Coles - deliberate knock-on, with Argentina awarded a penalty try (55 minutes)

Henry Pollock - offside following multiple infringements in quick succession from England (74 minutes)

Emmanuel Iyogun - offside, described as "cynical" by referee Angus Gardner (77 minutes).

Although England did not receive a yellow card against Fiji, Tommy Freeman and Guy Pepper were sent to the sin-bin against South Africa in their Nations Championship opener.

In the 2026 Six Nations, England received seven yellow cards while Henry Arundell was sent off against Scotland after receiving two yellow cards in the first half.

In addition to their four yellow cards, England gave away 14 penalties to Argentina's six, taking their tally for the Nations Championship to 34 conceded in three matches.

Former England wing Chris Ashton believes this pattern of ill-discipline is a weakness opposition teams will look to exploit.

"If you are playing against England, you know they are going to give penalties away and make some stupid errors that result in them losing games," Ashton told BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra.

It's a problem that's not lost on the players, with man of the match Immanuel Feyi-Waboso, who scored England's fifth try and lit up the match with his jinking runs, identifying it as something for the team to work on.

"Discipline is a huge thing," he said. "I don't know how many cards there were but too many. We can't be having games where we finish with 13 [players]."

Borthwick was eager to focus on his team's "incredible spirit" as they hung on to win.

"It's almost unfair to pull out one example but Henry Slade's tackle at the end was incredible," he said.

"It shows the fight that's in this group of players, as well as their togetherness and connection.

"Right now these players deserve a fantastic holiday, a good night out tonight and a good rest.

"They've worked hard and it's been a season that's been going a while.

"We all know about the number of minutes the English players play and these guys have stuck to it right to the very end."

Meanwhile, Argentina head coach Felipe Contepomi accused England of showing a "lack of respect" during his post-match news conference.

Contepomi took exception to the music and cheering that could be heard coming from England's changing room next door.

Having asked the England representative present in the room to stop the noise, Contepomi lost patience when it did not, and walked out of the room.

title: Cam Skattebo' moronic post-injury backflip may land him in hot water
description: Well, this is a great way to enrage the new Giants coaching staff.
fullText:

Skattebo is recovering from a "open tibia fracture and ruptured deltoid ligament in addition to a right ankle dislocation," per ESPN's Jordan Raanan, that ended his 2025 rookie season.

However, he showed off where he had surgery on his leg at Fanatics Fest right before doing the stupidest possible thing imaginable with said recovering leg. He attempted a backflip to showboat before tumbling to the ground.

Behaving like a doofus after such a major injury is... a great way to get yourself in hot water with your new coaching staff. We're sure new Giants coach John Harbaugh loves seeing Skattebo behaving like a toddler.

Kids, quite literally, don't try this at home.

title: 📹 After making history, Mbappé throws in the towel v Messi
description: In a completely crazy match, France lost to England 6-4 this Saturday (18) and had to settle for fourth place at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.+ 😅 At least it was fun! The internet reacts to the madness...
fullText:
In a completely crazy match, France lost to England 6-4 this Saturday (18) and had to settle for fourth place at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

+ 😅 At least it was fun! The internet reacts to the madness in England vs France

The result meant, among other things, France's worst World Cup result since Mbappé made his debut for the senior team.

In 2018, at the World Cup in Russia, the forward shocked the world by becoming a world champion at just 19 years old.

Then, in Qatar 2022, he had to endure finishing as runner-up against Argentina. And now, fourth place in 2026.

However, despite the disappointment of the semifinal defeat to Spain and the loss to England in the third-place match, Kylian Mbappé made history by pulling clear as the all-time top scorer in World Cup history with 22 goals in 22 matches.

The French Real Madrid star, however, after the match, "threw in the towel" in the personal battle he has been waging with Lionel Messi (21 goals) for the historic feat since the start of the tournament.

Mbappé not only believes Messi will score against Spain in this Sunday's final (19), but also revealed that he will be rooting for the Argentine No. 10.

"I would rather NOT be the top scorer and play in the final tomorrow. I don't care about being the top scorer. Messi will score tomorrow. He always does. They are two great teams, may the best one win. But I'll be rooting for Messi to have a great game, he deserves it."

Besides the battle to be the top scorer across all World Cups, Mbappé and Messi are also locked in a duel to be the top scorer of the 2026 World Cup.

title: ‘You don’t leave a club like that when you’re happy’ — Expert dismisses FC Barcelona rumors with Harry Kane
description: The FC Bayern star probably is not going anywhere.
fullText:
Rumors linking Bayern Munich striker Harry Kane with a move to FC Barcelona continue to make headlines, but former Chelsea, Real Madrid, and France midfielder Claude Makélélé believes there is little chance of the England captain leaving Bavaria anytime soon.

Speaking about the speculation, Makélélé pointed to Bayern Munich’s position as one of the world’s biggest clubs and questioned why the Bundesliga champions would ever consider selling one of their most important players.

“Harry Kane? He’s at a club where he’s flourishing, and you don’t leave a club like that when you’re happy. Bayern remain one of the biggest clubs in the world, and I don’t see them letting him go. And when I say Kane, I say Olise, too,” Makélélé told ComeOn. “A management would have to be, I won’t say stupid, but ignorant, to let players of that level walk away and strengthen a direct rival you might meet in the Champions League. You see what I mean? You keep your best players. In football anything can happen, but I don’t see a coach like Kompany letting his two key men leave. It would be suicide, practically.”

Makélélé’s comments echo the reality of Bayern Munich’s current situation. Since arriving from Tottenham Hotspur, Kane has become the centerpiece of Bayern Munich’s attack, consistently producing goals at an elite level while helping the club compete for domestic and European honors. Meanwhile, Michael Olise has quickly established himself as another key figure under Vincent Kompany, making the pair indispensable to Bayern Munich’s ambitions.

The former France international also raises an important point regarding the Champions League. Selling elite talent to another European heavyweight is rarely a strategy embraced by clubs with aspirations of winning the competition themselves. Allowing two cornerstone players to join a potential knockout-stage opponent would weaken Bayern while simultaneously strengthening a direct rival.

As Makélélé notes, “anything can happen” in football, but the circumstances surrounding Kane simply do not point toward a departure. Bayern Munich has little incentive to cash in on one of the world’s best strikers, and with Kompany building his side around players like Kane and Olise, any move to FC Barcelona appears highly unlikely at this stage.

title: Recent move by Saints should end any trade talks with Broncos surrounding all-time great
description: It seems as though the constant idea of Alvin Kamara coming to the Denver Broncos can be put to bed.
fullText:
Recent move by Saints should end any trade talks with Broncos surrounding all-time great originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Alvin Kamara is very likely going to play his entire career with the New Orleans Saints. Earlier this week, Kamara and the team agreed on a reworked deal that will keep him in New Orleans.

The Saints signed Travis Etienne in free agency, a move that is sure to change Kamara's role with the team, but he clearly still wants to play for the team that drafted him, where he is the franchise's all-time leading rusher.

Despite the Denver Broncos re-signing J.K. Dobbins this offseason and using a fourth-round pick to select Jonah Coleman, the idea of the Saints trading Kamara to the Broncos to reunite him with Sean Payton is constantly brought up. With the current state of each team's running back rooms, it makes sense for Kamara to stay in New Orleans, and it doesn't make much sense for him to come to Denver, even with the Payton connection.

This new deal with the Saints should end those talks for good.

"I thought this was a pretty good deal for Alvin Kamara and the Saints..

He wanted to stay in New Orleans" ~ @RapSheet#PMSLivepic.twitter.com/9sOHkewUh4

— Pat McAfee (@PatMcAfeeShow) July 17, 2026

Kamara has been as good as any running back in the league during his career, rushing for 7,250 yards and 61 touchdowns across his nine NFL seasons. He's been one of the best receiving running backs in the league during that time as well, catching 606 passes for 4,948 yards and 25 more touchdowns.

However, he is coming off what were clearly the lowest numbers of his career as he rushed for just 471 yards, averaging only 3.6 yards per carry in 2025. This could suggest that he is beginning to slow down and that is no doubt one of the primary reasons the team went out and got Etienne in free agency.

Kamara still has a big name and the thought of acquiring him can cause a stir on social media and excitement within the fanbase, but the Broncos won't have any interest in making that move at this point and it's likely that they never really did.

title: Game #98, Athletics vs. Nationals Game Thread
description: Time for a Saturday Night Victory
fullText:
Last night, the Athletics kicked off the second half the same way they finished the first half, suffering an embarrassing 23-4 defeat to kick off this three-game series against the Washington Nationals.

The team’s change at pitching coach did not provide an immediate boost, as several A’s pitchers combined to allow 18 runs in another disastrous performance at Sutter Health Park. According to OptaSTATS, during this 10-game skid, the A’s have made unwanted history, becoming the first MLB team to post an OPS that low (.575) while allowing an opponent OPS that high (1.019).

Tonight, the A’s can even this series and collect just their second win of the month. Before the game, the team made several roster moves following its latest humiliating loss.

We have made the following roster moves. pic.twitter.com/cjn6D3xlJO

— Athletics (@Athletics) July 18, 2026

Right-handed reliever Justin Sterner, who was one of two pitchers that allowed six runs yesterday, lands on the injured list with right knee chondromalacia, otherwise known as runners knee. If that was the cause of his recent struggles, hopefully his performance will improve once he returns from the injury.

The A’s recalled right-hander Geoff Hartlieb to take his place in the bullpen. First baseman Joey Meneses was designated for assignment to open a 40-man roster spot. The 34-year-old went 3-for-20 in nine games with the Athletics over the past few weeks. Now the club has five days to find a trade partner or place Meneses on waivers.

Speaking of trades, the A’s sent Aaron Civale, who had been designated for assignment a few days earlier, to the Chicago Cubs. The veteran pitcher returns to the team he finished the 2025 season with. In return, the “Green and Gold’ received minor-league pitcher Aiden Moffett, an undrafted free agent out of West Virginia. Moffett has struck out more than a batter per inning this season, but he has also issued 42 walks in just 22⅓ innings between the Arizona Complex League and Single-A.

The #Athletics have acquired Aiden Moffett from the Cubs for fellow right-hander Aaron Civale.

More on the 22-year-old 6-foot-3 reliever who pitched collegiately at Texas and LSU: https://t.co/E94So8Mput pic.twitter.com/9vLF2JF8bT

— MLB Pipeline (@MLBPipeline) July 18, 2026

Moving to tonight’s game, A’s right-hander J.T. Ginn will make his 19th start of the season. The 27-year-old enters his first outing of the second half with a 7-6 record, a 3.67 ERA, a 1.25 WHIP and 93 strikeouts over 103 innings.

Ginn ended his breakout first half on a sour note. He exited his start in Detroit after four innings because of an illness. A few days later, he imploded in the A’s final game before the All-Star break against the Chicago White Sox, allowing six runs in the bottom of the first inning and erasing his team’s early 1–0 lead.

This evening, the Athletics desperately need a quality start from Ginn. He must keep the ball down in the zone, trust his sinker to generate ground-ball outs and avoid mistake pitches. That will not be easy, as the Nationals have scored the most runs in the majors, but Ginn has proven to be the one starter the A’s can consistently count on when he takes the mound.

He will face off against Nationals’ right-hander Zack Littell, who is 7-6 with a 4.90 ERA through 19 appearances, including 12 starts, in his first season in the nation’s capital. Yesterday, the A’s offense struggled against Cade Cavalli’s overpowering arsenal. Littell presents a different challenge. With just 57 strikeouts in 90 innings, he relies on keeping hitters off balance and generating weak contact rather than overpowering them. That approach has paid off recently, as the 30-year-old has pitched well over his last three appearances, though he has earned just one win during that stretch.

Littell will face off against this A’s lineup:

Ginn on the mound 👊 pic.twitter.com/OwpCLwPhNq

— Athletics (@Athletics) July 18, 2026

While the team’s pitching has received most of the criticism, its offense has also failed to meet expectations.

Catcher Shea Langeliers needs to carryover his All-Star Game performance into games that actually matter. With Henry Bolte on the bench after a tough game in the series-opener,Lawrence Butler shifts to center field. Donovan Walton gets a second straight start at second base, while Jonah Heim and Jeff McNeil make their first starts of the series.

One of the club’s top prospects, Tommy White, is out of the starting lineup after making his MLB debut and collecting his first hit last night. Given the A’s record and the absence of Nick Kurtz, it is surprising to see Tommy Tanks not playing, but hopefully McNeil will prove the doubters wrong.

Ginn will go up against this lineup for Blake Butera’s Nationals:

late night nats lineup

→ https://t.co/iUBDqlACVs pic.twitter.com/VqI1OI1DKt

— Washington Nationals (@Nationals) July 18, 2026

For the A’s to have a chance to force a rubber match tomorrow, Ginn and the relievers who follow him must keep Washington’s offense in check.

title: Houston Rockets jersey history No. 21 - David Roddy (2025-26)

fullText:
The Houston Rockets have had players donning a total of 52 different jersey numbers (and have one not part of any numerical series for Houston assistant coach and general manager Carroll Dawson) since their founding at the start of the 1967-68 season, worn by just under 500 players in the course of Rockets history.

To honor all of the players who wore those numbers over the decades, Rockets Wire is covering the entire history of jersey numbers and the players who wore them since the founding of the team all those years ago right up to the present day.

With seven of those jerseys now retired to honor some of the greatest Rockets of all time to wear those jerseys, there is a lot of history to cover.

And for today's article, we will continue with the 16th of 16 who wore the No. 21, forward alum David Roddy. After ending his college career at Colorado State, Roddy was picked up with the 23rd overall selection of the 2022 NBA Draft by the Philadelphia 76ers.

The Minneapolis, Minnesota native played the first two seasons of his pro career with the Memphis Grizzlies after a near-draft night deal. He also played for the Phoenix Suns, Atlanta Hawks, and Philadelphia 76ers before he signed with the Houston Rockets in 2025. His stay with the team lasted until he was dealt in 2026 to the Atlanta Hawks.

During his time suiting up for the Rockets, Roddy wore only jersey No. 21 and put up 4.3 points and 1.7 rebounds per game.

fullText:
INDIANAPOLIS — Indiana Fever guard Caitlin Clark was assessed a technical foul in the first quarter of Saturday’s game against the New York Liberty. Clark now has six technical fouls on the season, and if she reaches eight, she’ll receive a WNBA-mandated one-game suspension.

Clark picked up a technical foul after she was leveled on a screen by Liberty center Jonquel Jones. The play was a no-call, but Clark took exception to the physicality and yelled at the closest official. Liberty star Breanna Stewart drained the ensuing technical foul free throw to give New York a 9-2 lead with 8:29 left in the first quarter.

Earlier this season, Clark said she wouldn’t change her approach after she picked up her fifth technical foul last month in a win over the Phoenix Mercury. In that contest, Clark was assessed a technical foul for loudly clapping in the direction of Mercury players during a skirmish, which Fever coach Stephanie White said the officials deemed as taunting.

“We should all just go on the calendar now and pick a game that I’m gonna be suspended for if I’m gonna get technicals for clapping,” Clark said after that game.

The Fever have previously appealed Clark’s technical fouls, but if this one is not rescinded, she’ll be tied with Mercury guard Kahleah Copper for the second-most technical fouls in the WNBA this season. Atlanta Dream forward Angel Reese leads the league with seven technical fouls.

This article originally appeared in The Athletic.

title: Reese Esponda Wins U.S. Classic Over Olympic Champion Jade Carey
description: Reese Esponda posted a career-best score to win the U.S. Classic on Saturday over Olympic champion Jade Carey. Former UCLA and elite star Katelyn Ohashi impressed in her first U.S.
fullText:
It’s time to put another gymnast on your radar. Reese Esponda won the U.S. Classic on Saturday in Hartford, Connecticut, edging out Olympic champion Jade Carey in a career-best effort. Esponda posted a massive 54.900 to win the all-around, defeating Carey by just short of a full point.

Esponda’s lead came in large part due to her meet-best floor routine, where the 17-year-old posted a huge 13.950 to win the gold medal. Carey, a three-time Olympic medalist, posted a strong 54.050 as she continued to ease back into elite competition after announcing her comeback.

The result marked a full-point improvement from Carey’s total at June’s American Classic. After an impressive all-around effort, Zoe Molomo rounded out the podium with the bronze medal.

World champion Skye Blakely competed on just bars and beam in Hartford, but the veteran showed impressive difficulty to finish in first place on both. Five-time World medalist Leanne Wong competed only on balance beam due to a recent wrist injury, posting a 11.900.

Katelyn Ohashi had to wait until nearly the end of the meet to make her debut, but the timing made no impact on the veteran gymnast. Starting on balance beam, Ohashi was confident and crisp in her second elite beam routine since 2013, nailing her flight series and dismount to one of the loudest crowd roars of the night.

“So cool, so cool," Ohashi stated, hugging her coach, Cale Robinson (Pacific Reign), following her routine. Ohashi received a 13.200 for her routine, finishing sixth in the event. Later on floor, Ohashi danced to a near two-point improvement from her score at June’s American Classic, earning her second 13.200 of the night.

While Ohashi’s combined total of 26.400 fell short of the 26.800 needed to qualify to August’s U.S. Championships, it displayed a remarkable improvement from her comeback effort at the American Classic.

Next up, the focus turns to Phoenix, Arizona. The nation’s top gymnasts will convene in the ‘Valley of the Sun’ for the 2026 U.S. Gymnastics Championships from August 7 to 9. In Phoenix, gymnasts will vie for spots on the U.S. National Team squad and for invitations to September’s World Team Selection Event.

From there, the USA Gymnastics selection committee will determine its six-gymnast team to compete at the 2026 World Championships in Rotterdam, the Netherlands.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

fullText:
Meadow Tian added to her impressive list of pre-college accomplishments Friday, July 17, by outlasting the field to win the 103rd Women’s Ohio Amateur Championship at Moraine Country Club.

The victory earned Tian a spot in the U.S. Women’s Amateur at The Honors Course in Ooltewah, Tennessee, on August 4-9.

Tian, who will be a freshman at Illinois this fall, began the week with rounds of 72 and 70 to place second in the qualifying rounds and enter the 32-player match play tournament as the No. 2 seed. She completed her quest with a 3 and 2 victory over Bowling Green’s Addison Kartusch in the 36-hole final.

Tian took the lead on the third hole and led by three twice until Kartusch won holes 13, 14 and 15 to tie the match. Tian regained the lead on 17. On the second 18, Kartusch forged ties on holes 2 and 5. But Tian regained control and led by four twice until both players parred 16. That left Tian up three with two holes to play and ended the match.

Tian, an Olentangy High School graduate, won the 2025 Division I individual state championship. In 2025, Tian also was the Southern Ohio Junior PGA champion, the Central Ohio district player of the year and the Ohio Girls Junior champion. In 2024, she finished second in the state high school tournament.

Tian reached the final with two dominant wins Thursday. In the quarterfinals, she defeated Furman’s Audrey Ryu of Dublin 4 and 2 and Columbus Academy senior Eva Lim 5 and 4.

Kartusch defeated Ohio University player and Centerville graduate Morgan Rodgers in Wednesday's first round of match play. Then she eliminated Sarah Willis, the former Penn State player and two-time state champion from Eaton.

On Thursday, Kartusch defeated Kent State’s Kristen Belden of Canton 3 and 1. Then she defeated Rutgers sophomore Elizabeth Ryu of Dublin 2 and 1 in the semifinals.

Zitzner seventh: Springfield 12-year-old Chaz Zitzner placed seventh at the Junior World Championships at Torrey Pines in San Diego on Thursday.

Zitzner fell off the pace on the first day with a 77 because of a quintuple-bogey 9 on his third hole and a double-bogey 6 three holes later. But he rallied with a

pair of 2-under par 70s to finish the tournament at 1-over par.

Yubo Shen of Thailand won the 11-12 age division at 9-under par with rounds of 72, 67 and 68.

Springfield City Amateur: Sixty-six players will tee it up Saturday on the Reid Park South Course in the annual Springfield City Amateur. The final round will be played Sunday.

title: Upcoming milestones: Mammoth players could reach a lot of benchmarks this season
description: Here’s every significant milestone that Utah Mammoth players are likely to hit in 2026-27.
fullText:
Now that the 2026-27 NHL schedule is out, here’s when each Utah Mammoth player is set to hit his next milestone.

Note that injuries, healthy scratches and other circumstances may delay the achievement of these benchmarks for some players, and offensive production milestones are projected based on the respective player’s recent history in those categories.

Playing your 1,000th NHL game is a big deal. Teams will typically hold an on-ice pre-game ceremony for the player and his family, highlighted by the presentation of a silver stick.

It’s also one of the few times in which a team is allowed to give a player an expensive gift. In most occasions, it could be considered cap circumvention, but the collective bargaining agreement states that as long as the gift doesn’t exceed $25,000, it’s allowed.

Ian Cole came 10 games shy of being the first player to hit that mark in a Mammoth sweater. With his departure to the Chicago Blackhawks, Anders Lee is now slated to become the first.

Provided he plays every game, the former New York Islanders captain will play his 1,000th game on March 26 in a home contest against the San Jose Sharks. Nate Schmidt will play his 900th game on the game day, assuming he plays every game as well.

Lee could also hit 600 points in 2026-27. He’s currently 51 shy of that mark.

No other Mammoth player is poised to hit 1,000 games in 2026-27, but most do have a milestone to look forward to. Keep in mind that the regular season will be 84 games long, rather than the 82 they used to play.

Of particular significance are the guys set to play their 400th games.

The NHL Alumni Association just unveiled a post-retirement wellness plan that guarantees dental and optical coverage for anyone who plays 400 games in the league. The program also guarantees a family doctor to anyone who has played a single NHL game, for those living in countries where health care is not automatically provided to its residents.

Nick DeSimone is two away from 100 (Oct. 3 @ CBJ).

Andrew Peeke is 14 away from 400 (Oct. 29 @ PIT).

Clayton Keller is 17 away from 700 (Nov. 5 @ MTL).

Kailer Yamamoto is 26 away from 400 (Nov. 25 @ MIN).

Nick Schmaltz is 30 away from 700 (Dec. 2 vs. WPG).

Vincent Trocheck is 32 away from 900 (Dec. 5 @ LAK).

Kevin Stenlund is 32 away from 400 (Dec. 5 @LAK).

Liam O’Brien is 35 away from 300 (Dec. 14 @ COL).

Karel Vejmelka is 38 away from 300 (Dec. 19 @ EDM, though goalies don’t play every game).

Barrett Hayton is 42 away from 400 (Dec. 31 vs. COL — the Winter Classic).

Lawson Crouse is 44 away from 700 (Jan. 5 vs. OTT).

John Marino is 57 away from 500 (Feb. 3 @ CGY).

Michael Carcone is 64 away from 300 (Feb. 24 vs. CGY).

Mikhail Sergachev is 70 away from 600 (March 9 vs. NYR).

MacKenzie Weegar is 71 away from 700 (March 13 vs. PHI).

Dylan Guenther is 73 away from 300 (March 18 @ ANA).

Nate Schmidt is 77 away from 900 (March 26 vs. SJS)

Jack McBain will hit 400 if he plays all 84 games (April 10 @ STL).

Three Mammoth players could realistically hit major goals milestones.

Dylan Guenther is nine goals short of 100.

Nick Schmaltz needs 25 more to hit 200.

Logan Cooley would reach 100 with 31.

A 31-goal season would be a career high for Cooley, but it isn’t unrealistic that he achieves it. He has shown steady progression year after year. He scored at a 36-goal pace last season, but missed 28 games due to injury.

Random fact of the week:
This game is on Dylan Guenther's birthday, April 10. In 2 career games on his birthday, Guenther has 2 goals and 5 points.

Last season, the Mammoth played on April 9 and 11, and he scored in both of those games.#TusksUp https://t.co/3oXCkIp05x

— Brogan Houston (@houston_brogan) July 17, 2026

Brandon Tanev: One more to hit 100.

Mikhail Sergachev: Four more to hit 300.

Tanev being that close to a milestone could be a motivating factor for him. With zero goals and just three assists in 56 games with the Mammoth last season, things didn’t go how he expected. He’s due for a bounce back, and an early celebration could lift some of the weight off his shoulders.

Who’s getting close to assist milestones? We’re glad you asked.

Nick Schmaltz is three shy of 500.

Clayton Keller is four shy of 600.

Kevin Stenlund is 10 shy of 100.

Michael Carcone is 12 shy of 100.

Dylan Guenther is 17 shy of 200.

Lawson Crouse is 41 shy of 300.

Barrett Hayton is 45 shy of 200.

Given how often Schmaltz and Keller assist on each other’s goals, it’s entirely possible — maybe even likely — they hit their respective milestones on the same goal.

On “32 Thoughts: The Podcast” this season, a fan asked who gets the puck in a situation like that. After all, players typically keep their milestone pucks as souvenirs.

The answer? It’s common for players to saw the puck in half so that each guy gets a slice of their personal history. That would be a cool keepsake to have.

Some guys take pride in their penalty minute totals. Here’s who could hit significant milestones this season.

title: Red Sox reactions: Wilyer Abreu’s heroics (another 2-HR game) help win streak reach 12
description: The Red Sox looked like they were headed toward the end of a long winning streak Saturday. Then, Wilyer Abreu took over.
fullText:
BOSTON — Instant reactions as the Red Sox (49-48) extend their winning streak to 12 games on the back of Wilyer Abreu, who hits two go-ahead homers — including the game-winner — in a 7-6 win over the Rays:

1) When Abreu gets hot, he gets hot. Less than 24 hours after a two-homer game in the nightcap of Friday’s doubleheader, Abreu put the Sox up twice — by a 3-2 score in the third, and then for good with a game-winning two-run shot over the bullpen in the seventh.

Abreu, who never got consistent chances against lefties before this season, continues to crush them in 2026. His first homer was off southpaw starter Ian Seymour and traveled 435 feet after leaving the bat at 108.5 mph. Four innings later, to cap off a four-run rally that flipped a big deficit into a lead, Abreu smoked a 103.7 mph, 412-foot shot off lefty Garrett Cleavinger.

Abreu became the first Red Sox player with two multi-homer games in a row in more than a decade. Mookie Betts was the last, back in 2016.

2) Boston’s 12-game winning streak is its longest in more than 20 years, with the last such heater coming in June 2006. Boston is back over .500 for just the second time all season; the other was on Opening Day when the club beat the Reds and got to 1-0.

The Red Sox were previously 0-34 when trailing by three runs in a game. Their first win under those conditions was an exciting one.

3) Trailing 6-3 entering the seventh, the Red Sox – who had just three hits to that point — came alive against relievers Cole Sulser and Cleavinger. Andruw Monasterio led off with a double, then Jarren Duran reached first when he dove to beat out a flip on a grounder to first. Masataka Yoshida grounded out to plate Monasterio from third and make it 6-4, and with two outs, Ceddanne Rafaela lifted an RBI double off the Monster to make it a one-run game. Abreu took over from there.

With the lead, Chad Tracy pivoted from some long relief options to his duo of top relievers. Setup man man Garrett Whitlock tossed a 1-2-3 eighth on eight pitches, but closer Aroldis Chapman lost the zone with two outs and walked back-to-back hitters with two outs. With two men in scoring position after a double steal, Chapman got Jonny DeLuca to ground out to end it.

4) Long man Ryan Watson got a rare chance to pitch, something he hadn’t done much in July. Watson had logged just three innings this month (and one outing since July 1) before coming in to relieve Sandoval in the sixth. With the Sox trailing by two, the rookie got five quick outs before DeLuca rocketed a solo homer into the Monster seats to stretch the score to 6-3. He kept the Red Sox in the game, though, and picked up his first big league win in the process.

5) Most of the wins during the streak have come on the backs of great performances by Boston’s starting pitchers. That wasn’t the case Saturday behind an uneven Patrick Sandoval.

Making his first start as a member of the Red Sox at Fenway Park, Sandoval looked good early before Tampa Bay’s offense started teeing off. Sandoval lasted five innings as the Rays scored five runs (four earned) on nine hits. In a three-run fourth, the Rays had a single, three doubles and a homer — and it could have been worse if the Red Sox didn’t get the final out at the plate in the inning on a nice relay from Rafaela to Anthony Seigler to Carlos Narváez.

6) Left fielder Jahmai Jones made an instant impact with the bat in his first start since coming over in a midweek trade with Detroit. The journeyman tied the game in the second (at 2-2) with a two-run, no-doubt homer over the Green Monster that left the bat at 105 mph.

It was an adventure for Jones on the other side of the ball, though. Playing the Green Monster is a learning curve for any left fielder, but he looked lost on doubles by Chandler Simpson and Richie Palacios in the fourth and got tripped up in the corner on another play earlier in the game. Both Simpson and Palacios scored as part of a three-run fourth for Tampa Bay.

Jones’ eventful day ended very early — in the fourth — when Tracy pinch-hit Duran for him against righty reliever Kevin Kelly.

7) The Red Sox had played crisp, clean baseball on a consistent basis for a few weeks but didn’t execute at the same level Saturday. Boston had two errors that led to Rays runs and lost another opportunity to score on the basepaths before coming back late.

Overaggressiveness by Willson Contreras led to two runs in the second. With a man on second the speedy Simpson looking to bunt, Contreras charged so hard he was about 15 feet from the plate when the pitch was delivered, and that backfired when Simpson swung, chopped a hard grounder toward second base and Seigler threw the ball into the dugout because Contreras wasn’t back in time. Ryan Vilade scored on the play and Simpson later raced around to make it 2-0 on a Nick Fortes bloop RBI single.

After Abreu’s first homer, Simpson doubled and Narváez airmailed Caleb Durbin on a throw trying to nab him on a stolen base attempt at third. Simpson jogged home to the the game.

In the fourth, Romy Gonzalez stole second after getting hit by a pitch, then took off for third and was thrown out rather easily. Monasterio then lifted a ground-rule double that would have scored Gonzalez if he had been on second.

8) It’s easy to take Rafaela’s defense for granted, but he made a stellar play — even by his high standards — in the third. With one out, DeLuca lifted a soft liner into shallow center and Rafaela, after getting a perfect jump, made a fantastic diving play coming in on the ball.

The Gold Glover’s awareness was again on display in the fourth when DeLuca hit a rocket double into right center and Rafaela corralled it before throwing a strike to Seigler, who gunned down a chugging Díaz at the plate on a close play.

9) Rain looked like it was going to be a factor all afternoon with a poor forecast around Fenway, but once play started — at 5:11 p.m., after a 61-minute delay — there wasn’t a drop to be found until the seventh inning.

10) Watching Junior Caminero hit is a sight to behold as he crushes just about everything thrown to him. He hit balls at 101 mph (double play), 105.1 mph (groundout) and 114.4 mph (groundout) in a 1-for-4 day.

11) Sunday’s series finale will bring a very good pitching matchup with righty Sonny Gray (11-1, 2.54 ERA) set to pitch for the Red Sox and lefty Shane McClanahan (8-5, 2.83 ERA) pitching for the Rays. First pitch is at 1:35 p.m. ET from Fenway.

title: Nashville Predators Offseason Recap
description: In this series, Heavy Sports is looking at what every NHL team has done over the offseason. Next up is the Nashville Predators. The Predators had an average season last year as they finished with a 38-34-10 record. The 86 points in the standings were not good enough for a spot in the postseason. As […] The post Nashville Predators Offseason Recap appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
In this series, Heavy Sports is looking at what every NHL team has done over the offseason. Next up is the Nashville Predators. The Predators had an average season last year as they finished with a 38-34-10 record. The 86 points in the standings were not good enough for a spot in the postseason. As a result, former general manager Barry Trotz stepped down from the role into an advisory position. Ex-Colorado Avalanche GM Chris McFarland takes over at the helm of the club’s management team. McFarland has been busy this summer working the phones, having made plenty of moves thus far as he looks to get this team back into playoff contention next season.

Key additions: Jack Ahcan, Chase Bradley, Mavrik Bourque, Ross Colton, Jack Drury, Adam Edstrom, Nils Hoglander, Alex Kerfoot, Ilya Lyubushkin, Hunter Skinner

The Predators have made a few sneaky additions to their forward core. The biggest add of note is Mavrik Bourque from the Dallas Stars. Bourque should get an increased role with this team compared to what he had on a crowded Stars offensive rotation. Bourque signed a six year $33 million contract extension after being acquired by this team. McFarland has conducted plenty of business with his former Colorado organization, acquiring forwards Ross Colton and Jack Drury from the team. They should add depth to Nashville’s bottom-six. Nils Hoglander and Alexander Kerfoot have also been inserted into the offense. On the back-end, Ilya Lyubushkin has been added to the mix.

Key subtractions: Magnus Chrona, Andreas Englund, Kevin Gravel, Erik Haula, Zachary L’Heureux, Tyson Jost, Jordan Oesterle, Fedor Svechkov

Nashville has avoided any major losses this summer. The most notable exit is forward Erik Haula, who heads off to the Los Angeles Kings. Haula produced 14 goals and 24 assists for 38 points in 81 games with the Preds last year. Fedor Svechkov and Tyson Jost are a couple more secondary forwards who have departed from this team.

Overall, it has been a productive opening session for McFarland with his new Predators team. According to The Athletic’s model, Nashville is the most improved squad of the offseason thus far. We’ll see if the results translate on the ice in what projects to once again be a loaded Central division picture next season. There is no doubt the offense has made gains; the question mark for this team is on the blueline. Beyond star defenseman Roman Josi, the defense leaves a lot to be desired with a lack of depth. Of course, having Jusse Saros in net helps make up for the defensive shortcomings. It will be interesting to see if McFarland adds more support on defense to aid Josi by the time next season gets underway. Much of the Predators success next season will hinge on their top veterans such as Steven Stamkos, Ryan O’Reilly and Filip Forsberg remaining in strong form. The hope is that their assembly of young players can take a step forward as well.

All transactions are available via Puck Pedia.

title: Could Red Sox Actually Pull Off Once Unthinkable Feat To End Season?
description: The Boston Red Sox trail by seven games in the American League East going into Sunday
fullText:
Could the Boston Red Sox actually do the unthinkable and win the division?

Their 12-game win streak has them back in the wild card race, but they still trail by seven games in the division. Seven games is a daunting hill to overcome, but the team is red hot, and the two teams in front of them cannot say the same.

The Yankees have struggled ever since Aaron Judge got hurt, and his return is up in the air. He is still shut down from baseball activities, and the Yankees are sliding. They have a five-game lead over the Red Sox.

Then there is the Tampa Bay Rays, whom the Red Sox will try to sweep on Sunday. The Rays are a good team, but they certainly have their weaknesses. Of the Yankees, Red Sox and Rays, the Rays have the easiest remaining schedule, and the Yankees have the toughest.

Looking for the perfect gift for a baseball player in your life? Check out our brand new baseball lifestyle brand!

The Red Sox could actually find themselves in second place in the division by the end of August if the Yankees continue to struggle without Judge. After that, it would be a mad dash to the finish line against the Rays.

The Red Sox still have four games remaining against the Rays and Yankees. Winning those matchups will be key to winning the division. Additionally, both of those series will take place after the midway point of August. There is a slim chance Garrett Crochet or Roman Anthony will be back by then. Ideally, both would be back for the final month of the season.

Another highlight is the polo collar, a feature of classic football shirts.

title: Why the World Cup half-time show may be a taste of things to come
description: The 2026 World Cup's spectacle is creating debate about the place of music in sport - and how it could infiltrate other tournaments.
fullText:
Half-time at a football game is normally a chance to grab a drink or snack, make a mad dash for the toilets or vent with friends about some questionable refereeing or defending.

But at this year's World Cup final between Spain and Argentina, for the first time in the tournament's history, there will be a half-time show - complete with performances from Madonna, Shakira, BTS and Justin Bieber.

It's already caused controversy in the sporting world as the performance means the break will last up to 25 minutes - when the International Football Association Board (Ifab) states players are entitled to a maximum 15-minute stoppage.

The show will take place on Sunday night at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey and has been described by governing body Fifa as a "landmark celebration at the intersection of sport, music and global impact".

Does a performance like this, during the most-watched sporting event in the world, herald something similar coming soon to a stadium near you? And do fans want that?

It is expected that both the BBC and ITV will screen the full show, with punditry filling the gap while the stadium staging is put up and taken down.

At last year's Club World Cup final, which was also organised by Fifa and held in New Jersey, there was a 24-minute half-time featuring live music from Coldplay, J Balvin, Doja Cat and Tems.

Sports broadcaster Betty Glover, who has been covering the World Cup across the US for the last month, worked at last year's Club World Cup final in the same stadium.

"There's a battle to get more Americans involved and fall in love with this game - it's obviously not their main sport, but when you're walking around speaking to people, more are getting on board with it," she tells the BBC.

James Massing, whose team at Live Nation is responsible for putting on the World Cup final half-time show, says "expectation from fans has heightened" when it comes to big sporting events, meaning music could feature 

He tells the BBC the sports industry needs to innovate because it is "competing with any other live experience - going to the museum, going to the theatre, going to the cinema".

Massing, who has also worked on the Super Bowl and the Club World Cup final show, feels adding a musical performance makes "a final feel like a final".

While watching The Killers perform ahead of the Uefa Champions League final between Arsenal and Paris Saint-Germain in May this year "you felt the energy in the stadium peak," he adds.

But British football content creator Ellis Platten is "not a fan" of some initiatives at this year's World Cup, including the half-time show or the hydration breaks.

The hydration breaks take place 22 minutes into each half and have been used to show adverts on some US networks.

"I get that it's the World Cup final, but it's the most-watched event in the world already and I don't think you need to add these things to it," he tells us.

"Being a football fan in a stadium, by the end of half time people are quite restless."

Platten, 28, has been travelling around the host nations to watch World Cup games for the last month.

He says he is "curious to see how the half-time show affects the game in general".

Ifab have previously rejected proposals to extend football half-times beyond 15 minutes, citing concerns over player welfare and the potential for injury after prolonged inactivity.

"You'd almost have to make a sub at half time because of player welfare," Platten notes.

He adds: "They're not thinking about that, they're just thinking 'oh we can have Justin Bieber on stage' but Messi's in a World Cup final - I think he's slightly more important."

Glover agrees and says she is "not sure football needs" a half-time show.

"The World Cup this year feels very American, I'm watching coverage in the US and you've got so many adverts, they barely do any analysis," she says.

Massing says "there is no compromise when it comes to the integrity of the sport" and adds that "player welfare is absolutely fundamental" when organising half-time shows.

Coldplay's Chris Martin has curated the World Cup final performance - and it will feature Nigerian star Burna Boy. According to Fifa, the acts are not being paid but the event will raise money for an education fund.

It will conclude a North American World Cup where celebrities have been seen at every turn - from delivering the match ball to waving to cameras after flashing up on big screens during games.

Musical performances at sports events are nothing new - American football's Super Bowl has had a half-time show since 1967.

But in the UK and Europe the uptake has been slower - the first musical performance at the Champions League was 10 years ago, while the British Grand Prix started adding music to its F1 offering at Silverstone three years ago.

American sports appear to be setting a precedent - international NFL games, taking place in mostly European cities, added specific half-time shows to all games in 2025.

"What is starting to happen is a lot of sports are becoming more global," Massing says.

When that happens, there is naturally a shift to try and make that event "an occasion and a moment rather than a game," he adds.

Platten fears that following Fifa's move, half-time shows could become a part of domestic football - something he says would be unnecessary.

Glover is not so sure about whether the concept will catch on and says she "can't imagine this happening in Spain, Portugal and Morocco for the next World Cup".

For her, "If entertainment attracts new fans without compromising the football itself then it is a positive thing."

title: Kyle Juszczyk, George Kittle replies to Deebo Samuel spark reunion talks

fullText:
NFL wide receiver Deebo Samuel Sr. is a free agent after his one-year deal with the Washington Commanders expired.

Samuel, 30, is coming off a 2025 season in which he tallied 72 catches on 99 targets for 727 yards and five touchdowns.

He might be in a tough spot as an aging receiver, who maybe lost a step, but what does fare well for him are his consistent numbers. He has caught at least 50 passes for at least 600 yards in the previous five consecutive seasons.

Samuel doesn't seem stressed about his NFL future. He has optimal faith that he'll land somewhere. He took to social media to express his thoughts.

title: Fatima Kline dominates Tabatha Ricci at UFC Oklahoma City
description: The UFC Oklahoma City main card featured a women's strawweight bout between contenders Tabatha Ricci and Fatima Kline. Ricci entered the match ranked No. 8 in the division and looking to rebound from a decision loss to No. 2 ranked Virna Jandiroba. Kline made the walk to the octagon inside Paycom ...
fullText:
The UFC Oklahoma City main card featured a women's strawweight bout between contenders Tabatha Ricci and Fatima Kline.

Ricci entered the match ranked No. 8 in the division and looking to rebound from a decision loss to No. 2 ranked Virna Jandiroba. Kline made the walk to the octagon inside Paycom Center riding a three-fight winning streak and ranked No.11. "The Archangel" was hoping to pick up the biggest win of her career and climb in the rankings.

Ricci came out aggressive. She crossed the cage and pressured Kline. Ricco moved into the pocket with punches and looked for a takedown. Kline shook off the attempt. Ricci clinched and tossed Kline but Kline gained top position on the ground. Ricci got to her feet but was slammed back to the canvas. Ricci worked her way to her feet and the two separated. Kline changed levels and took Ricci down with a double-leg. Ricci got to her feet in the closing seconds. MMAWeekly scored the round 10-9 for Kline.

Ricci took the center of the cage in the second. She pressed forward but Kline met her with punches. Ricci ducked under a kick and worked for a takedown. Kline immediately scrambled to her feet. Ricci looked for another takedown but Kline defended it and gained top position. Ricci rose to her feet and circled away. Midway through the frame, they exchanged right hands. Kline defended another takedown attempt. Ricci connected with a combination. Kline fired back. Ricci clinched but couldn't get Kline down. Kline tripped Ricci to the ground and took her back. She looked for a rear-naked choke in the closing seconds. MMAWeekly scored the round 10-9 for Kline.

Kline took the center of the cage and landed a right hand to start the final frame. She connected with a heavy right hand. Ricci continued to press forward. Kline suplexed Ricci to the ground. Ricci got back to her feet and the two exchanged right hands. Kline found a home with a clean right hand. Kline began landing regularly. Ricci fought back but was outclassed. Ricci secured a takedown with just under a minute remaining on the clock. Kline scrambled free and reversed the position. She mounted Ricci at the bell. MMAWeekly scored the round 10-9 for Kline.

The judges scored the fight for Kline via. unanimous decision. All three scorecards read 30-27.

fullText:
WEST PORTSMOUTH – Todd Gilliland made sure not to take all the credit.

“It makes me happy for them that our success has helped them along the way,” Portsmouth West’s sixth-year football coach said July 14, referring to a trio of his former assistants now entering their first season as head coaches elsewhere. “But they’ve earned everything they got, because those guys are top-notch coaches.”

In so doing, Gilliland illustrated the point each of his former sideline sidekicks made.

Notre Dame’s Steven Irwin, Minford’s Josh Bailey and South Point’s Brian Wroten are the manifestation of Gilliland's coaching tree beginning to sprout.

Each did their time with Gilliland’s Senators. Each touted the culture he's built on the West Side and consider themselves better equipped to run their own programs because of being part of it.

“He has established a clear standard, and the players completely buy into it," Bailey said. "Because of that player buy-in, the school and the community fully rally behind the program.

“On top of that, he surrounds himself with a dedicated staff who are willing to do whatever it takes to compete at the highest level. When you are part of an environment with that kind of alignment and work ethic, it naturally prepares guys to step up and run their own programs.”

Gilliland puts assistant coaches in position to grow, Wroten and Irwin each said, by giving them meaningful tasks and responsibilities.

“Coach Gilliland empowers his assistants,” Wroten said. “He doesn't just assign jobs, he teaches you why things are done and gives you opportunities to lead. That prepares coaches to think like head coaches instead of just position coaches.”

Concurred Irwin: “Todd does a great job of empowering his staff, while holding the entirety of the program together. It helps knowing your head coach has the utmost confidence in you to do a great job. Then, if you make a mistake, you know Todd will be there to help you figure out a solution together and continue to build.”

Area programs with coaching vacancies have found that what West has done under Gilliland is worth trying to replicate. The Senators are 38-21 in five seasons under his direction, with the crown jewel in that tenure an 11-2 2022 campaign that culminated in a Southern Ohio Conference Division II title and third-round postseason appearance.

That preceded consecutive eight-win seasons in 2023 and ‘24, and though West slipped to six victories last fall, it has won a playoff game in each of the last two years, a program first.

“It is great to see that Todd, staff and players throughout the years have created a program that others in the area want to possibly emulate through new hires,” Irwin said. “The brand of West Side football that players and staff created in recent years is something that is special, and it shows.”

Bailey’s connection with Gilliland dates back the furthest of the trio. He played for Gilliland at Sciotoville East. He also worked one season on Gilliland’s staff at West between stints as an assistant at Minford.

He’s also the only one who will have to coach against his old boss in 2026. Minford is scheduled to host the Senators in an SOC II game on Sept. 25.

It’ll be their third meeting on opposite sidelines, Bailey said.

"I honestly look forward to it, though," Bailey said. "I have so much love and respect for the West staff and their players, so getting to compete against them is always a highlight."

Irwin, Bailey and Wroten are each entering their first season running their own squads, while former Senators assistant Trenton Fike went with Irwin to the Titans. Gilliland is excited to follow their progress.

"We always hate to see those guys go, but at the same time, I think you want to have people on your staff that have aspirations of being a head coach one day or growing in their career," Gilliland said. "It's exciting to see that around the area, the guys I've been able to hopefully have a part in their growth."

"When you see former assistants leading programs across the region, it speaks to the investment he made in the people around him, not just the players,” Wroten said of Gilliland. “Every coach is going to put their own personality and philosophy into their program, and I've certainly done that at South Point, but there are lessons I learned from coach Gilliland about organization, preparation and building relationships that I'll carry with me throughout my career. It's rewarding to see that influence spread throughout southeastern Ohio.”

Real American Freestyle (RAF) returns TONIGHT (Sat., July 18, 2026) with its eleventh event, streaming live from inside UW-Milwaukee Panther Arena in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.

No. 2-ranked Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC) Lightweight contender, Arman Tsarukyan, and three-time Welterweight title challenger, Colby Covington, finally collide in RAF 11’s main event.

In the co-main event, former UFC fighter and two-time NCAA Division I national wrestling champion, Ben Askren, returns to the wrestling mats against former UFC Welterweight champion, Belal Muhammad. It was literally less than one year ago that Askren “died five times” and required a double lung transplant (scary details here).

In addition, Frankie Edgar vs. Clay Guida also compete against each other.
